data.title: Crescent Energy: A Successful Example Of Divestitures And New Acquisitions
data.description:
Crescent Energy (CRGY) is an American company dedicated to the exploration and production of oil and gas in the United States. The company operates primarily in the Eagle Ford Basin in Texas and the Uinta Basin. Over the past period, Crescent has significantly increased its margins and productivity through the divestment of low-producing wells and the subsequent acquisition of higher-yielding acreage. This has simultaneously enabled strong FCF generation and improved the company's current financial ratios.

Thesis

As mentioned in the introduction to this report, Crescent Energy operates in three specific basins in the United States. First, in the state of Texas, the company operates in the Permian and Eagle Ford basins, one in the west and the other in the south of the state. Third, Crescent operates the Uinta Basin, located between the states of Colorado and Utah. Over the past few years, the company has divested some acreage and acquired new acreage. This strategy appears to have been based on managing its assets according to the profitability ratios of the wells in these basins relative to the company's overall profitability.

Crescent divested assets based on the premise that they had an EV/YTD Ann. EBITDA ratio greater than 5.5x, an average YTD percentage of oil of 29%, and an average annual production of 41 MBOE/d.

In fact, to mention some of the deals in that process of increasing the productivity of the operating basins, it was announced on December 3 that the company closed the sale of non-operated assets in the DJ basin for $90 million. This deal adds to the divestments of Barnett and Rockies that the company had already reported, exceeding a total value of over $900 million. As the company explains, the acres were acquired by a private company and represent a production potential of 7 MBOE/d that CRGY was not exploiting anyway.

Another point I'd like to mention, as I consider it extremely important for understanding the company's current situation, is the performance of some key economic and financial indicators. Firstly, as shown below, thanks to this strategy of divesting unprofitable assets and acquiring profitable ones, the company has significantly improved its EBITDA margin.

Secondly, a significant decrease in production breakevens, measured as adjusted OPEX per BOE, can be observed. In its latest earnings report, Crescent reports a drop from $12.86 per barrel to $11.50 per barrel.

Another key aspect that makes the company extremely attractive, in my opinion, is its growth in free cash flow (FCF). As shown below, the company's CAGR growth has exceeded 30% since 2021, reaching an annualized value based on the first three quarters of 2025. Crescent's FCF value increased from $277 million to an annualized value of $822 million by 2025. While I intend to delve deeper into the ratios that the company shows based on the latest results in the next segment, it seems relevant to note that with these latest values, Crescent's P/FCF is around 3.03x.

In that sense, finding a company that shows the margins that Crescent demonstrates, and in line with that, the FCF generation I described earlier, is an extremely attractive proposition. Especially when we add a management team that is focused on improving profitability through divestments and more favorable acquisitions with that money.

And to conclude this general overview of the company, I would like to mention that this asset management policy enabled a significant and favorable restructuring of the company's debt. Specifically, it extended the maturities of its corporate bonds relative to the average of its main competitors. It is noteworthy that, in an environment where interest rates had risen due to the Fed's contractionary monetary policy, Crescent was able to extend the duration of its bonds.

To conclude this section of the thesis and further illustrate the advantages of the company, which are the reason I consider it a STRONG BUY, I want to briefly discuss the two main basins it operates: Eagle Ford and Uinta.

Eagle Ford

As is well known, the Eagle Ford Basin is located in southern Texas, near the Permian Basin. The company operates in this area, producing 168 MBOE/d, with 41% of that production being oil. This operation has an estimated capital expenditure of $180 million in relation to what it was in Q3 2025, which shows the strong profitability that this location presents for the company.

Uinta

Turning to Uinta, the operation generates the equivalent of 24 MBOE/d, with over 60% of that being crude oil. Furthermore, the capital investment required for the basin in Q3 2025 was $12 million. While proportionally lower, the Uinta Basin offers attractive profitability and margins for the company.

3Q2025 Results

In this section, I intend to provide a brief summary of the results published by the company in 3Q2025 and compare them with previous quarters. To begin, I would like to mention Crescent's oil and gas production during this period. As shown below, oil production averaged 103,000 barrels per day (Bbls/d), while natural gas production averaged around 631 million cubic feet per day (MMcf/d). Adding these two products to LNG, Crescent generated an average of 253,000 barrels of oil equivalent (BOE/d) in 3Q2025. Compared to the previous quarter, this represents a slight decrease from the 263 MBOE/d of 2Q2025. However, compared to the same quarter of 2024, there is a significant increase from the 219 MBOE/d of that period. It is worth noting that this increase is due to the aforementioned divestment process undertaken by the company.

Now I'd like to talk about the financial variables. In that regard, the company's revenue for 3Q2025 was $866.6 million. Compared to $744.7 million in the third quarter of 2024, this represents a year-over-year increase of more than 16%. The figure is even higher when we look at the revenue for the first nine months of the year. For the full year 2025, the company reported $2,714.7 million, compared to $2,055.6 million in 2024. This represents a 32% increase.

Regarding EBITDAX, we see a similar trend. While Crescent reported a value of $486.5 million in the third quarter of this year, the value for the same period in 2024 was $430.4 million. This represents a 13% increase. However, if we look at the cumulative figures for the first nine months of the year, we see that the increase is significantly greater. The cumulative total for 2025 shows that the company generated $1,529.8 million, which represents an increase of more than 43% compared to the $1,063.5 million of 2024.

It's interesting to note that if we annualize this accumulated value to 2025 and compare it to the company's EV at the time of writing this report ($5.72 billion), we can see that the EV/EBITDAX ratio is around 2.8x. From my perspective, this value is extremely attractive, even for a company in the oil and gas industry, which already shows values ​​below the S&P 500 average.

The last point I want to highlight regarding the results is the Net Cash from Operating Activities that the company reported. Looking at Crescent's latest earnings release, the company reported accumulated cash from operating activities for the first nine months of the year of $1.309 billion. Compared to $838.6 million in 2024, the year-over-year increase is over 50%. In fact, if we annualize the 2025 amount and compare it to the company's market capitalization ($2.49 billion), we see that the P/CF ratio is just 1.43x. This means that if this level is maintained, the company could generate cash equivalent to its entire market capitalization in just a year and a half. This undoubtedly reinforces my STRONG BUY rating.

Valuation

In this section, I intend to present a target price for Crescent Energy based on a valuation using multiples with comparable companies. To select the companies I used as comparables, I relied on the list shown in the Peers section of Seeking Alpha. This list includes oil and gas companies operating in the United States, with a market capitalization between $2 billion and $3 billion. Among the selected companies are CIVI, BTE, NOG, and BKV.

As on previous occasions, I decided to divide the analysis into two distinct segments. First, the valuation itself, where a target price for the company is calculated based on a comparison with the sample using three ratios: P/E, EV/EBITDA, and P/CF. The second part of the analysis compares Crescent to the other companies and the sample average using a series of economic and financial variables to contextualize the company's situation. In this sense, and beyond the results obtained from the ratios, the aim of this second part is to gain insight into Crescent's economic and financial positioning relative to the other selected companies, in order to delve deeper into its potential.

Before presenting the results, I would like to provide some context regarding the methodology and assumptions used to construct the valuation model. As previously mentioned, the P/E, EV/EBITDA, and P/CF ratios were used for the selected companies. For each of these ratios, I calculated a value for the sample based on an average of the selected companies weighted by market capitalization. While the size of each company is relatively similar, given the sample's composition, I felt it was important to give greater relative weight to the larger companies.

Once I obtained the average ratios for the sample, I calculated the target price for each one specifically for Crescent. This resulted in three values, which were then averaged according to the weighting factors shown in the table below. As you can see, I decided to give more weight to the EV/EBITDA ratio because I consider it the most representative of the industry in relation to the overall industry capital structure.

Based on these selected companies and the assumptions I used, it's clear that Crescent has strong upside potential relative to the sample data I used. Even considering that the industry's overall ratios are low compared to other industries that make up the S&P 500, Crescent shows growth potential that, from my perspective, is extremely interesting.

Moving on to the second point, to characterize Crescent's relative position compared to the other companies in the sample, I selected EBITDA Margin, EBITDA CAGR, and Net Debt to EBITDA as the economic and financial indicators. As can be seen in the table below, the company is quite well-positioned relative to the average. In fact, relative to the average, only in the net-debt-to-EBITDA ratio is it worse than the average.

Therefore, it's clear that the company not only has considerable upside potential, but also demonstrates a certain economic and financial advantage over the other companies in the selected sample. All of this supports the STRONG BUY rating I gave it in the thesis section and reinforces what I explained there.

Risks

In conclusion, I would like to mention the main risks I see in this investment. On the one hand, many of the ratios, results, and margin improvements we've described are based on the successful deals the company has made recently. The divestment of unprofitable wells and the acquisition of better acreage have led to the aforementioned productivity increase. Therefore, it is a risk if the company starts making deals that negatively impact that productivity. On the other hand, a drop in the international price of oil and a return to the downward trend seen in 2024 could undoubtedly erode the margins and FCF generation the company has demonstrated, compromising the investment thesis.

data.title: Oracle: This Is A Golden Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Shares of Oracle Corporation (ORCL) slumped 11% on Thursday after the Cloud company reported weaker-than-expected top-line results for its second fiscal quarter. The revenue miss was not large, but concerns over the company's CapEx levels have started to weigh on Oracle's shares. The Cloud company signed a historic $300B Cloud computing deal with OpenAI in September, and it raised its CapEx guidance by $15B to $50B for FY 2026 which is making some investors uneasy. However, market intelligence firm Gartner recently confirmed strong growth in the Data Center market expected in 2026, so I believe the drop is an overreaction to an otherwise solid earnings report and a buying opportunity for long-term investors seeking Cloud exposure.

Previous rating

I rated Oracle a buy in my previous work—Unleashing Massive Cloud Growth—and highlighted especially the potential for market share growth as Oracle is a relatively small player with a 3% market share. Since then, Oracle announced a $300B five-year Cloud computing deal with OpenAI (OPENAI)… which is pursuing a $850B Data Center buildout plan of its own. In the second fiscal quarter, Oracle continued to see surging RPO growth which indicates strong demand for the Cloud company’s platform services. Additionally, Oracle is very profitable and with projections calling for continued growth in the Data Center market, I believe renewed concerns about the firm's CapEx spending are exaggerated.

Strong Cloud growth creates an attractive investment setup

Oracle generated $16.1B in revenue in the second quarter—ending November 30, 2025—showing 14% year-over-year growth. Despite this strong growth, the Cloud platform’s second-quarter results missed the top-line estimate by approximately 1%.

Despite the exaggerated reaction to the firm's results, Oracle's earnings report card was solid and showed growth momentum, especially in the Cloud segment… which is where the platform generated $8.0B in revenues and saw 34% Y/Y growth. In other words, Cloud, which has emerged as a major growth driver for Oracle, is growing more than twice as fast as the company is overall.

Oracle continued to do very well in terms of its performance obligations as well which grew to a record $523B as of the end of Q2 '26, showing 438% year-over-year growth. On a sequential basis, Oracle added a massive $68B to its order backlog due to new commitments from Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), highlighting that the company is adding contracts faster than it can deliver on its services. RPO growth is a strong performance metric for software/SaaS companies that are signing new deals with enterprise customers and it shows that the company is benefiting from rising deal velocity.

Oracle's Cloud and AI-driven business operations yielded a second-quarter profit of $6.15B, showing 95% year-over-year growth. In the last three years, Oracle's earnings growth has significantly exceeded its top-line growth, highlighting operating leverage potential for Oracle as it works through its remaining performance obligations.

No concerns about AI-driven growth

Gartner is a market intelligence firm that projects that the Data Center market will continue to see healthy expansion in 2026, although the company also projects that (CapEx) spending will slow down. For the next year, Gartner estimates that Data Center systems spending will increase by 19% year-over-year, highlighting a favorable demand driver for Oracle's Cloud services. Oracle guided for $50B in CapEx spending this year (+$15B) which is a level that some investors are worried about giving inflated valuations in the sector. However, given the massive increase in revenue projected and the solid outlook for the Data Center market, I believe the positives widely outweigh the negatives here.

In terms of CapEx spending, Oracle is outperforming other hyperscalers like Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), or Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) by a massive margin.

According to Oracle's October analyst presentation, the Cloud enterprise projects 31% average annual top-line growth until the end of the decade at which point its revenues are to hit $225B.

This growth is driven by three major factors:

  • Growing AI adoption and deployment across the enterprise landscape. AI models are becoming more potent and the suite of enterprise AI products and services is growing steadily. As AI workloads continue to soar, more Cloud computing power, provided by companies like Oracle, will be needed
  • OpenAI is pushing the entire Cloud market into a new IT upgrade cycle: the company is pursuing an $850B buildout of Data Center infrastructure with companies like Oracle, Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) and Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) set to grow alongside the ChatGPT creator
  • Oracle already has a strong position in the enterprise software market which the company can leverage to its advantage. Enterprise AI and workflow automation are two major growth areas that I see the Cloud computing platform doing well in going forward. I have discussed the enterprise (agentic) AI opportunity in broader terms in my work on Snowflake: Wait For A Drop.

Oracle’s valuation

Shares of Oracle slid 11% after the Q2 '26 earnings report, which represents a buying opportunity. RPO and top-line growth strongly indicate that Oracle’s business is on a good foundation and developing in the right direction. The fact that Cloud is growing twice as fast as the firm's consolidated revenues shows that the platform can transform robust demand for Cloud services into double-digit revenue growth. Further, Oracle is widely profitable and has a ton of earnings momentum, which is the result of the company's aggressive take on AI/Cloud investments.

Currently, shares of Oracle are trading at a price-to-earnings ratio, based on next year's projected earnings, of 24.9X which makes them the cheapest Cloud investment in the big tech group. Amazon, Alphabet, and Microsoft are trading at forward price-to-earnings ratios of 29.3X, 28.1X, and 25.8X. Microsoft put up strong numbers in the last quarter as well and outperformed its rivals in terms of Cloud-specific revenue growth: Microsoft generated a massive 40% Azure growth (28% Intelligent Cloud growth) and this outperformance is why I see a path to $600 for the big tech company.

Oracle, I believe, could trade closer to a ~35X forward P/E ratio given its massive expansion in RPO and sizzling Cloud platform business, with which the enterprise taps into the red-hot Data Center market. A 35X P/E ratio, applied to a consensus EPS estimate of $7.97 (Oracle's FY 2027), calculates to a fair value of the company's shares of $279, leaving 40% of upside to be realized.

A 35.0X P/E ratio could also be justified based on high valuations for companies like Broadcom (34.0X P/E) or AMD (32.0X P/E). Companies that have exposure to the rapidly evolving Data Center market can easily trade at a price-to-earnings ratio way above 30.0X in my opinion. Oracle's shares already traded as high as 41X earnings after the company announced its landmark deal with OpenAI in the third quarter. The recent correction, in my opinion, is a golden opportunity to buy a surging Cloud player at a significantly discounted price.

Risks with Oracle

Slowing growth in remaining performance obligations and in the core Cloud business are two potential risks for investors who want to buy the drop. Further, Oracle is growing its RPO mainly due to massive contracts with OpenAI, so if one of those contracts falls through—potentially due to lack of available financing or overestimated deal benefits—Oracle may see a considerable compression of its RPO and valuation factor going forward.

Final thoughts

The post-earnings selloff for Oracle's shares is likely exaggerated and most likely not deserved. In no way, in my opinion, does the top line misreflect any valid concerns about the company's growth trajectory or Data Center CapEx trends. Oracle’s revenue backlog, RPO, continued to surge in the second quarter due to the OpenAI contract, highlighting strong demand for the company's Cloud infrastructure services. Oracle is massively profitable, which in itself lowers risks for investors.

I like Oracle because of its low 3% share in the Cloud computing market, which gives the enterprise a long growth runway as well as underdog status in 2026. Investors looking for a long-term Cloud growth play may want to buy Oracle as long as shares are this heavily discounted.

data.title: How Nvidia Grew Data Center Market Share In Q3
data.description:
In Nvidia Corporation’s (NVDA) fiscal 2026 Q3, Nvidia’s share of the overall data center software and equipment market grew to over 50%. This was largely due to the fact that demand for AI processing has moved beyond the individual server to rack-scale assemblages of AI accelerators. These AI accelerator racks can perform as unified single accelerators through hyper-fast networking known as “scale up” networking. Nvidia pioneered this approach and currently has little effective competition for these systems.

In fiscal 2026 Q3, Nvidia’s share of the overall data center equipment market grew to over 50% for the first time

Nvidia’s Data Center Segment revenue grew by 66% y/y to over $51 billion. To put this in perspective, in just one quarter Data Center added $10 billion in revenue, greater than Advanced Micro Devices’ (AMD) total company revenue in calendar Q3.

Fiscal Q3 restored the Data Center revenue growth trend that has persisted since fiscal 2024 Q2:

The slope of the revenue line since fiscal 2024 Q2 has remained largely constant. I believe this is due to Nvidia being limited by supply constraint, especially wafer allocations by TSMC (TSM).

Most of the growth in the overall data center software and equipment market was due to the $10 billion increase in Nvidia’s Data Center segment:

In the process, Nvidia gained about 5 percentage points of share to cross the 50% threshold for the first time:

Nvidia’s lack of AI competition in “scale up” is visible in the ML Perf training benchmark

Nvidia’s share gains are due to the lack of competition in rack scale AI accelerators, exemplified by Nvidia’s NVL72 system:

Scale up networking is provided by the fifth generation NVLink Switch which can provide nearly instantaneous connectivity between any of the 72 Blackwell GPUs in the rack at a data bandwidth of 1,800 GB/sec.

No other accelerator architecture offers this kind of “scale up” connectivity in the world today. Nvidia began working on scale up for AI training, when it first became apparent that a small cluster of GPUs would not be able to handle the work load.

Now, with the advent of “thinking” AI models, the workload for inference processing has increased by 100 fold or more, requiring similar scale up for inference. Data centers have mostly lost the option of smaller AI servers for inference processing.

Scale up has become the new battleground between the various aspirants to AI acceleration. But as I pointed out in AMD’s Missing (Ultra Accelerator) Link, AMD and other members of the Ultra Accelerator Link consortium are still working on their alternative to the NVLink Switch.

The lack of competition for NVLink Switch and NVL72 systems is very apparent in ML Commons benchmarks. ML Commons publishes objective, third party benchmarks for AI accelerators. However in ML Perf Training, only two companies are represented, Nvidia, and AMD.

There used to be more companies represented, such as Intel (INTC) with its various AI accelerators (Ponte Vecchio, or Habana Labs Gaudi) or Google with its Tensor Processing Unit. But these have apparently fallen by the wayside.

But more striking is the difference in scale up. The ML Perf Training benchmark has results for as many as 5,120 Blackwell GPUs as multiples of NVL72 rack systems, down to as few as 8 GPUs. The largest tested group of AMD GPUs is just 16.

Prospects for future competition: Ultra Accelerator Link

A number of recent announcements have raised hopes for effective competition with Nvidia. Perhaps one of the most interesting is the recent acquisition of startup Celestial AI by Marvell (MRVL) for $3.25 billion. The acquisition announcement had some interesting things to say about “scale up”:

AI is reshaping data center architectures at unprecedented speed. Next-generation accelerated systems are no longer confined to a single rack – they are evolving into multi-rack configurations connecting hundreds of XPUs with an integrated high-bandwidth, ultra-low latency, any-to-any scale-up fabric. This architecture allows each XPU to access the memory of every other XPU directly. These advanced fabrics demand purpose-built switches and protocols such as UALink, engineered to deliver the performance and efficiency required at scale.

Given the power, bandwidth, latency, and reach requirements of multi-rack scale-up fabrics, interconnects will increasingly transition to all-optical connections. This acquisition positions Marvell to lead this technology shift and capture a brand-new semiconductor TAM for optical interconnects.

The first paragraph is the usual belated realization of how important scale up has become. But there’s also the recognition of the importance of the switch in the scale up architecture. Apparently Marvell wants to take that on as part of the UA Link consortium.

But the fact that Marvell had to spend $3.25 billion for an optical interconnect startup shows how desperate Nvidia’s competitors are. The focus of the UA Link consortium has been primarily on defining the link. That’s the easy part.

The hard part is architecting a switch that can achieve the scale up objective, which is that performance scale roughly linearly with number of GPUs. This isn’t easy. In the old days of SLI and Cross Fire, adding a second GPU only increased gaming performance by roughly 50%.

The ML Perf Training benchmarks show variable scaling, but there’s always a law of diminishing returns. If you use 9 times the GPUs, you don’t get 9 times the performance. Benchmark performance tends to be 60-70% of ideal for scaling within an NVL72 rack.

Scale up performance depends mostly on the efficacy of the switch in welding the many GPUs into an effective whole. That’s where I think the UA Link consortium is going to run into difficulties, just by virtue of lack of experience.

As I’ve pointed out, by late next year, when AMD plans to produce its first rack scale system, Helios, Nvidia will be on its 6th generation of NVLink. Even if the UA Link consortium manages to deliver a usable switch in time, I doubt it will match the performance of NVLink 6 in real world AI workloads.

As such Nvidia will still be in a leadership position in AI acceleration and be able to command a premium price for its products, as it does now. But maybe not as much of a premium.

Investor takeaways: Nvidia is an excellent investment, even assuming slowing data center growth

Lately, there’ve been a number of announcements of potential competitors or competing products to Nvidia. I think it’s great. I welcome competition.

There’s no way that Nvidia is going to be left alone in the data center AI market the way it is now. But I don’t think that means Nvidia is going to suffer a decline in data center revenue in the next five years, either.

By virtue of various causes, including increased competition and sated data center demand, Nvidia’s rate of growth will undoubtedly slow. And the AI data center TAMs that are being tossed around of trillions of dollars per year are just crazy.

Fortunately for investors, Nvidia stock is not overpriced, even assuming slowing data center growth. In my 5 year Discounted Cash Flow model for Nvidia, I project that Data Center growth will start to roll off after fiscal 2027:

The cumulative Data Center revenue for the model period (fiscal 2026-2030) is projected to be $1.522 trillion, with maximum Data Center revenue in fiscal 2030 of $374 billion. This implies that the data center software and equipment TAM (not just AI) is never more than about $600-800 billion per year, depending on what you want to assume for Nvidia’s market share.

The DCF calculated fair value is $257.95 for the following revenue and EBITDA profile over the model period:

So, I’m inclined to think that Nvidia is undervalued. There may be a bubble in AI growth expectations, but there hasn’t been a bubble in valuations. Investors have been a little too cautious about Nvidia. I remain long Nvidia and rate it a Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: This Is How I Shield My Retirement Portfolio With Covered Call ETFs
data.description:
Seeing the title of this article, some of you might probably think that I am seriously missing something about how the covered call ETFs work. Usually, whenever we have had larger drawdowns, most of these vehicles have followed the declining market. In addition, the fact that covered call ETFs tend to yield above 10% does not make the case for portfolio protection stronger. We could even make a case that it makes things even more suspicious.

I totally get it and there is certainly a fair amount of truth in such a thinking. For example, if we look at some of the more established covered call ETFs that have been operational for more than 5 years - e.g., the Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD) and the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) - we will see that all of them have lost notable value in pronounced market decline situations.

Let's take the most recent correction that we had back in early April, this year:

We will see the same pattern for other popular covered call ETF names as well: such as the Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX), the Neos S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI), the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI), etc.

It is important to understand that not all covered call ETFs are created equal (although, it might seem so by looking at the largest and most popular ones). And by the word "equal" I mean that the lion's share of covered call ETFs are effectively tied to either S&P 500 (SPY) or Nasdaq-100 (QQQ), which are AI and Mag-7 biased indices. So, if these two start to melt down, the chances are very high that standard covered call ETF investors would see their portfolio prices and income stream stumble as well.

Having said that, let me unpack the less explored world of covered call ETFs, which is where, in my view, durable (or retirement) income investors can access two things: 1) shelter from the AI-induced plunge and 2) stable 10%+ income streams.

Covered call ETF shield against AI bubble

I will first level set our understanding of covered call ETFs and their value creation mechanism. In this case, I will be talking about out-of-the-money covered call ETFs, which is where the majority of new ETF supply is concentrated and where ~90% of my covered call ETF portfolio is located.

In this context, I really like how the TappAlpha SPY Growth & Daily Income ETF (TSPY) - one of my long positions - manager has visualized the essence:

It is clear that due to the capped upside that stems from the sold call options on a specific underlying that the ETF has chosen to track, investing in covered call ETFs are inherently inferior products, when the market is trending upwards.

We can see how this has worked out in practice (i.e., the index beating the covered call ETFs, which base their strategies on it):

Now, there are obviously some environments, which are quite favorable for covered call ETF products:

  1. When the market trades sideways, where collected call premiums should warrant alpha (but given how the market usually performs, I would say that completely flat scenario is only theoretical).
  2. When the market nosedives, where the collected call premiums should provide slight downside protection relative to fully open downside that is associated with any pure-play index move (but still, this is an unpleasant scenario and probably not acceptable for those, who want to keep the values stable).
  3. When the market is slightly rising or declining, where the covered call ETFs can both capture the upside and enjoy full benefits of option premiums that should secure alpha or neutralize the correction risks (sweet spot case).

In my view, given where we are in the market cycle (almost 3-year long bull market with average annual return of ~20%, which is significantly exceeds the long-term average of 10%), I consider the three aforementioned scenarios quite realistic.

Having said that, let me take a step back.

At the end of the day, what matters for me and I would say many other retirement (durable) income focused investors is not whether alpha is captured or not. Instead, the key thing is to secure durable passive income streams that are not funded by the underlying NAV or principal erosion.

And here there are two relevant risks that we have to appreciate as covered call ETF investors:

  1. Reduced income streams from compressed volatility levels.
  2. Steep correction after which the markets remain depressed for quite long period, which would ultimately shrink the underlying's asset base from which covered call ETFs could be written (i.e., also an income reduction case).

Now, regarding the first risk, I am not worried at all. The past couple of years we have lived in a relatively low vol environment in which covered call ETFs have still managed to deliver double digit yields. Furthermore, currently, we are not in an elevated volatility world, which would render the yields abnormally high and more likely subject to correction as the vol normalizes. The S&P VIX Index (VIX) chart below captures this nicely:

However, the second risk is where I would spend more time and try to understand how to navigate that.

The thing is that if any of the major indices like SPY or QQQ enter into free-fall and after hitting the bottom remain lying on the ground for a prolonged time period (at least more than 1 month, which is the usual period for OTM call options), then the chances would be very high that most covered call ETFs would experience unpleasant income reductions.

Here in this chart we can see how JEPI's dividends sharply dropped in early 2021 (the lagged effects kicked in from the COVID-19 driven sell-off). Then another drop happened in early/mid 2023, which stemmed from the 2022 bear market:

Granted, from all of the situations JEPI has managed to recover due to booming markets.

Yet, the question remains: what would happen if there was not a subsequent recovery (at least not as fast and extensive). The answer is that most probably these covered call ETF investors would have to live with significantly smaller dividends.

This is not a risk that I want to take.

The only way to shield the portfolio income streams is through introducing a better downside protection than what the overall market (in this case QQQ and SPY) has. The only way to get it in place is by selecting covered call ETFs, which are not linked to QQQ or SPY and embody some characteristics that might be beneficial in the case of AI drawdown.

The way how I am expressing this view is by going long a diversified blend of defensive covered call ETFs. For example, I have integrated the NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI) - exposure to physical gold - in my portfolio to access protection from rising fiscal deficit and damaged Fed independence driven sell-offs. I have bought the Neos Real Estate High Income ETF (IYRI) as well to get an exposure to real estate, which should offer better protection if the inflation dragon comes back. Similarly, the NEOS Enhanced Income 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (CSHI) allows to achieve an enhanced yield by having the downside risk tied to the U.S. T-bill risk. Finally, the NEOS Russell 2000 High Income ETF (IWMI), which I have included in my portfolio, can shield the portfolio if small and mid cap equities remain flat or do not get as hurt as the AI related indices.

The bottom line

Most covered call ETFs do not bring any defensive to the table. So, if the SPY or QQQ plunge, the lion's share of ETF world would very likely follow. And if after the correction there is no immediate recovery, the chances would be quite high of experiencing structurally reduced passive income flows.

For most durable and retirement income investors (including myself), it is not an option.

The way to mitigate that is by tilting the covered call ETF portfolio towards specific vehicles that run their covered call overlay strategies on other underlying indices than SPY or QQQ.

As elaborated in the piece, a blend of IAUI, IYRI, CSHI and IWMI would, in my humble opinion, position income investors well for having their cash flows protected from a potential AI-driven sell-off.

data.title: IonQ Vs. Rigetti: The Quantum Pair Trade Hiding In Plain Sight
data.description:
In quantum computing, narratives and valuations make it difficult to call out absolute valuation-fundamentals gaps and time entries. I have favored QTUM in the past because it reduced idiosyncratic risks within the traded quantum stocks universe. However, a better risk-aware approach is a pair trade that should ideally ensure that relative fundamentals eventually play out and holding shorts becomes technically hedged to an extent, enabling longer-term positioning. Regardless of whether quantum stocks are in bubble territory or if narratives and potential continue to drive share prices further up, a well-established pair trade should make for an ideal setup.

IonQ, Inc. (IONQ) and Rigetti Computing, Inc. (RGTI) make for an ideal pair for many reasons. First, they are both fundamentally pre-economic, far from profitability and burning cash. Secondly, both firms rely heavily on government, defense, and academic ecosystems to subsidize their development. Lastly, both IonQ and Rigetti are pursuing universal gate-based quantum computing, not annealers or analog simulators (like D-Wave)—making them a natural pair trade within the same quantum sub-segment.

Fortunately, my thesis finds the relative fundamental valuation gap overwhelmingly in favor of IonQ, making the long IONQ and short RGTI a high-conviction setup. The gap is substantial, and the markets should see that too—hence investors entering into this setup should be able to have the appetite for a longer ride and remain invested if RGTI keeps testing the setup.

Completely Different Technology Momentum

IonQ has moved out of the lab and now claims 99.99% two-qubit fidelity. Fidelity determines how long a quantum circuit can run before noise overwhelms the computation. Every extra decimal point counts toward the higher ranges, and by a significant order of magnitude. At 99.99%, IonQ is now operating at a level where some practical advantage becomes possible in optimization and simulation tasks. According to management commentary in the Q3 2025 earnings call,

So our physical qubits are now substantially higher performance than anyone else's logical qubits. So in the short term, for the near-term road map, you can take our physical qubit count as you look at other people's logical qubit counts.

Rigetti, by contrast, is at 99.5% today and guiding to 99.7% in 2026 and 99.8% in 2027. That sounds close on paper, but is vastly different in practice. A move from 99.5% to 99.7% roughly doubles usable circuit depth; 99.7% to 99.8% is another doubling. But IonQ’s 99.99% gives another ~20x deeper circuit window, which translates directly into whether real applications (chemistry, complex optimization) run to completion.

This is because expected error grows roughly as "circuit depth * (1–fidelity)", the maximum usable depth scales as "1 ÷ (1–fidelity)". So 99.5% (error 0.005) allows ~200 layers, 99.7% (~0.003) allows ~330, and 99.8% (~0.002) allows ~500—but 99.99% (0.0001) jumps to ~10,000 layers, which is where real algorithms stop collapsing into noise. DARPA explicitly did not advance Rigetti to Stage B, stating they need improved plans for error correction and coupling — the two pillars of full-scale machines. IonQ, in contrast, did reach Stage B.

In terms of scalability, IonQ’s 2026 machine targets 256 qubits manufactured on mature semiconductor nodes. This enables supply chain resilience and lower costs as established foundries open up for manufacturing. Rigetti's roadmap requires increasing chip sizes and ultimately demands a new fab because their 150mm Fremont line cannot reach the uniformity required for ~99.9% fidelity. In other words, IonQ avoids this capex entirely by designing for standard manufacturing. Rigetti hits a capital wall as soon as it tries to cross into fault-tolerant territory.

Commercial Traction

IonQ raised guidance for FY2025 to $106-110m. Rigetti reported $1.9m for Q3 (down more than 20% YoY). IonQ's revenue scale is already at least 10x of Rigetti's, yet the market cap is only 2-2.5x of what Rigetti commands. It is probably simplifying the story too much simply comparing market caps with revenue numbers in early-stage companies, but there are nuances in revenue quality as well.

IonQ now has platform-based recurring revenues trickling in, national security contracts and multi-year system integration deals. Rigetti's revenues are primarily government and grant-driven at the moment. The biggest developments announced in Q3 were two 9-qubit system sales totaling $5.7m and a $5.8m AFRL contract recognized ratably over 3 years. Overall, IonQ's deployments are closer to real-life commercial workflows, while Rigetti is being used primarily for evaluation and R&D.

What Valuations Mean

IonQ has $3.5B in cash after its October raise and no debt. Rigetti has $600M, and no debt. However, runway needs appear to be vastly different. I feel IonQ has run its roadmap with lower capex requirements. Rigetti must spend hundreds of millions on a new fab or port its technology to a foundry. Despite the capital need differences, a net-of-cash value measure (simply cash as % of market cap) shows IonQ's cash reserves at 18% of the market cap. Rigetti's cash reserves (including equivalents) account for only ~6.5% of the market cap.

Strictly looking at the price-to-forward sales ratio, Rigetti is now trading at ~1200x levels compared to IonQ's less than 200x range. While the gap isn’t as simple as one stock being overvalued and the other cheap, it reflects two very different revenue bases and levels of market traction. But combining the valuations with the fundamental differences discussed, IonQ definitely seems to have a lower gap than Rigetti at today's levels.

The Pair Trade Implementation

The fundamentals-valuations gap is apparent. So what are we missing here if the pair trade of Short Rigetti and Long IonQ is as obvious as it is. The most difficult part of an IONQ-RGTI pair trade is not the fundamentals, but the path implications. Rigetti has a long history of insane rallies often 200-300%. IonQ does not move as much with more defined metrics to measure progress against, versus the narrative-based play in Rigetti.

This means being directionally right is not sufficient. Investors who identify the opportunity may still lose despite being right. The first thing we have to remove is shorting Rigetti outright—a 200-300% rally in a matter of a few weeks could challenge that setup. Quantum stocks often see borrow disappear during momentum spikes, triggering involuntary covering at the top. Also, the magnitude of moves is so different between the two stocks that the IonQ hedge may often not compensate enough—imagine a scenario when Rigetti is 200% up and IonQ is only up by 50%.

As implied volatility is very high, far OTM options with far expiries also trade at costs that may mean losses even if the underlying stock moves strongly in the right direction. A Rigetti $40 call expiring on the 23rd of January, 2026, is trading at ~4% of the current stock price. That implies Rigetti needs to move ~50% within a month or so to start hitting positional profits. Even if it tests $40, you may lose the option premium. This could add up over time. Such setups could be particularly harsh if both stocks consolidate for a long period while you bleed setup costs.

The best way to implement the pair trade is to size RGTI appropriately. Looking at the magnitude of price moves historically, $1 long IONQ for every $0.25–0.40 short RGTI looks like an appropriate balance. This ensures even a 300% move in RGTI drags the setup by ~75% to ~120%. IonQ’s expected long-duration compounding still offsets the shock, avoiding margin calls and the need to exit at peak mania phases. Other active measures such as altering position sizes, and reacting to trends, could also help allow IonQ to outrun Rigetti eventually, even if the trade takes a few months to a couple of years to play out. Combining positions with far OTM options as insurance could also work, depending on risk tolerances.

data.title: Record August World Oil Production
data.description:
A guest post by Ovi

The focus of this post is an overview of World oil production along with a more detailed review of the top 11 Non-OPEC oil-producing countries. OPEC production is covered in a separate post.

Below are a number of Crude plus Condensate (C + C) production charts, usually shortened to "oil," for the oil-producing countries. The charts are created from data provided by the EIA’s International Energy Statistics and are updated to August 2025. This is the latest and most detailed/complete World oil production information available. Information from other sources such as OPEC, IEA, STEO and country-specific sites such as Brazil, Norway, Mexico, Argentina and China is reported to provide a one- or two-month outlook.

The World’s August oil production increased by 405 kb/d to 85,244 kb/d, a new World Peak Oil All-Time High. Last month's projection for August’s production was 85,465 kb/d, too high by 221 kb/d.

This chart also projects World C + C production out to December 2026. It uses the December 2025 STEO report along with the International Energy Statistics to make the projection. Production in September is projected to increase by 1,439 kb/d to 86,683 kb/d; if correct, another new World Peak Oil next month. The large September C + C increase is due to the 1,165 kb/d increase in crude production shown in the crude graph. That increase then drops in December. The large drop is just as surprising as the increase.

The 12-month highest CMA shown at March 2026 is 85,547 kb/d vs. the September 2018 12-month CMA of 82,962 kb/d.

For December 2026, production is projected to be 85,519 b/d, a downward revision of 146 kb/d from last month.

August’s World oil output without the US increased by 318 kb/d to 71,450 kb/d. September’s production is expected to increase by 1,388 kb/d to 72,838 kb/d.

The projection is forecasting that December 2026 oil production will be 72,006 kb/d, an increase of 556 kb/d from August.

A Different Perspective on World Oil Production

August’s Big 3 oil production decreased by 24 kb/d to 32,917 kb/d. Saudi Arabia contributed a 100 kb/d drop to that decrease, while the US added 86 kb/d. While August’s drop was largely due to Saudi Arabia, OPEC’s increasing production, which started in April 2025, will continue up to December 2026 and will reverse this unexpected one-month drop.

Production in the remaining countries has been slowly increasing since the September 2020 low of 42,970 kb/d. Production in August 2025 rose by 429 kb/d to a new high of 52,327 kb/d.

Countries Expected to Grow Oil Production

This chart was first posted a number of months back and shows the combined oil production from five Non-OPEC countries, Argentina, Brazil, Canada, Guyana and the U.S., whose oil production is expected to grow. These five countries are often cited by OPEC and the IEA for being capable of meeting the increasing World oil demand for next year. For these five countries, production from April 2020 to July 2024 rose at an average rate of 1,130 kb/d/year, as shown by the orange OLS line.

To show the impact of US growth over the past 5 years, U.S. production was removed from the five countries, and that graph is shown in red. The production growth slope for the remaining four countries has been reduced by 614 kb/d/yr to 516 kb/d/yr.

August production has been added to the five growers chart, up by 439 kb/d to 24,449 kb/d. Of the 439 kb/d, Guyana added 250 kb/d, U.S. added 86 kb/d and Argentina added 44. For the Five growers W/O U.S., August production rose to 10,655 kb/d, up 146 kb/d from July.

Note: The OLS lines are updated to July.

World Oil Countries Ranked by Production

Above are listed the World’s 13th-largest oil-producing countries. In August 2025 these 13 countries produced 78.6% of the World’s oil. On a MoM basis, production decreased by 55 kb/d in these 13 countries, while on a YOY basis, production rose by 2,053 kb/d. Note the YoY increases from Brazil, Kazakhstan and U.S.

August Non-OPEC Oil Production Charts

August's Non-OPEC oil production increased by 452 kb/d to 55,179 kb/d. September is expected to add an additional 58 kb/d to 55,237 kb/d.

Using data from the December 2025 STEO, a projection for Non-OPEC oil output was made for the period August 2025 to December 2026 (Red graph). Output is expected to grow by 361 kb/d to reach 55,540 kb/d in December 2026.

August’s Non-OPEC W/O US oil production increased by 366 kb/d to 41,385 kb/d. September’s production is projected to add 7 kb/d to 41,392 kb/d.

From August 2025 to December 2026, production in Non-OPEC countries W/O the U.S. is expected to increase by 642 to 42,027 kb/d.

Non-OPEC Oil Countries Ranked by Production

Listed above are the World’s 11 largest Non-OPEC producers. The original criteria for inclusion in the table was that all of the countries produced more than 1,000 kb/d. Both Oman and Angola were below 1,000 kb/d for a few months but have rebounded to above 1,000 kb/d.

August’s MoM production decreased by 21 kb/d to 46,061 kb/d for these eleven Non-OPEC countries, while as a whole, the Non-OPEC countries saw a yearly production increase of 2,485 kb/d to 55,179 kb/d. Major yearly gains came from Brazil, Kazakhstan and U.S.

In August 2025, these 11 countries produced 83.5% of all Non-OPEC oil.

Non-OPEC Country’s Oil Production Charts

Angola’s August oil production dropped by 70 kb/d to 1,035 kb/d. I think the large drop is associated with the extra-large 200 kb/d increase reported last month by the EIA. According to the National Agency for Petroleum, Gas and Biofuels (NAP), July production was 999 kb/d. For August, the NAP reported production was 1,035 kb/d, same as the EIA.

According to the NAP, October production rose to 1,066 kb/d, red markers.

The EIA reported that Brazil’s August production decreased by 65 kb/d to 3,894 kb/d.

Brazil’s National Petroleum Association (BNPA) reported that production rose slightly in September and then rose by 116 kb/d in October to 4,025 kb/d, a new record high. The pre-salt blue graph tracks Brazil’s trend. Pre-salt production increased by 100 kb/d in October to 3,307 kb/d, also a new high.

Canada’s oil production decreased by 61 kb/d in August to 5,049 kb/d.

A projection has been made for September production based on preliminary production provided by the Canada Energy Regulator (CER). September’s projected production dropped to 5,024 kb/d. The CER estimate contains some NGLs, which have been removed from the projection. On average the EIA reduces the CER’s monthly production by 370 kb/d.

The EIA reported China’s August oil output rose by 30 kb/d to 4,300 kb/d.

The China National Bureau of Statistics reported September production rose to 4,325 kb/d and then in October dropped by 85 kb/d to 4,240 kb/d.

On a YoY basis, China’s August production increased by 101 kb/d from 4,199 kb/d.

According to the EIA, Kazakhstan’s August oil output increased by 65 kb/d to 2,147 kb/d.

Since Argus no longer reports OPEC+ crude production, production data for Kazakhstan will now be taken from the monthly IEA reports. In September 2025, pre-salt crude production rose to 1,920 kb/d, an upward revision of 80 kb/d from the IEA’s October report. In the IEA’s December report, they are reporting October’s production dropped by 230 kb/d to 1,690 kb/d and then rose to 1,800 kb/d in November.

The November oil production is 320 kb/d above the OPEC+ target set for Kazakhstan.

According to the EIA, Mexico’s August output dropped by 4 kb/d to 1,735 kb/d.

In June 2024, Pemex issued a new and modified oil production report for Heavy, Light and Extra Light oil. It is shown in blue in the chart, and it appears that Mexico is not reporting condensate production when compared to the EIA report.

In earlier EIA reports, they would add close to 55 kb/d of condensate to Pemex’s “Total Liquids” report. More recently, the EIA has been adding 90 kb/d of condensate to Mexican production. For September and October production, 90 kb/d have been added to the estimate. October’s production is estimated to be close to 1,731 kb/d. Note that Mexico’s production, as reported by them for the last four months, has stabilized around 1,650 kb/d, according to Pemex.

The EIA reported Norway’s August production dropped by 6 kb/d to 1,974 kb/d.

Separately, the Norway Petroleum Directorate (NPD) reported that October’s production dropped to 1,837 kb/d, red markers.

The Norway Petroleum Directorship also reported that October’s oil production was 0.4% above forecast.

According to the November OPEC MOMR: "Throughout the rest of the year, production is expected to be supported by the Johan Castberg and Jotun FPSOs, alongside robust volumes from Johan Sverdrup."

According to the EIA, August’s output rose by 5 kb/d to 1,008 kb/d and appears to have begun a budding growth phase. Previous production peaked in October 2022.

Qatar is now providing the EIA with monthly updated oil production back to January 2025. Qatar’s August output was reported to be 1,322 kb/d, up 8 kb/d from July.

The EIA reported Russia’s August 2025 C + C production decreased by 10 kb/d to 9,803 kb/d and was up 124 kb/d from August 2024.

Up to August 2025, Argus Media used to report OPEC+ crude production. That monthly report has now been discontinued. The above chart now also shows Russian production as reported by the IEA. It is difficult to assess the accuracy of the IEA report, but over the last few months the IEA’s Russian production has been around 100 kb/d to 150 kb/d higher than Argus Media's. The best that can be done at this time will be to compare the production trends between the EIA and the IEA. I think that Russian oil production continues to be a major state secret at this time because of the damage being caused by the heavy bombing to its crude oil processing-related facilities.

According to the IEA December report, October crude production was 9,240 kb/d, and then November production dropped by 210 kb/d to 9,030 kb/d. November production was 500 kb/d lower than the OPEC+ target set for Russia and is the first month that may be showing the effects of Ukrainian bombing.

This US production chart up to August 2025 is the same as the one published last week in the US update post. However, the projected portions of the two production graphs have been updated using the December 2025 STEO.

U.S. September projected production has been revised up by 74 kb/d to 13,869 kb/d.

Production in December 2026 is expected to be 13,513 kb/d, 92 kb/d lower than shown in last week’s US update. Production peaks in October 2025 and then begins a slow decline.

Note production in the Onshore L48 is essentially flat from July to December and begins to decline.

Oil production in Guyana has been flat since January 2024. However, for August 2025, production rose by 250 kb/d to a new high of 895 kb/d.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
